original_hypothesis.title: Genesis.participate Per-Transaction Cap Not Per-User: Whales Can Dominate Allocation
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Genesis.participate checks virtualsAmt <= maxContributionVirtualAmount per transaction but does NOT check the cumulative mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender]. A user can call participate() multiple times with amounts up to maxContributionVirtualAmount each, accumulating far more than the intended cap. This defeats the purpose of limiting individual contributions.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The variable name 'maxContributionVirtualAmount' strongly implies a per-user cap, but the implementation only enforces a per-transaction limit. The cumulative mapping is incremented with += but never checked against the cap.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(virtualsAmt <= maxContributionVirtualAmount) checks single tx, not cumulative mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender] + virtualsAmt

preconditions: User has sufficient VIRTUAL tokens and allowance

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Per-tx cap instead of per-user cumulative cap
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender] += virtualsAmt accumulates without limit check

analysis_questions: Is maxContributionVirtualAmount intended as a per-user or per-transaction limit?
How does off-chain distribution handle users who over-contributed?
Can a whale receive disproportionate agent tokens?
validation_requirements: Call participate() twice with maxContributionVirtualAmount, verify total exceeds cap
Check if off-chain distribution normalizes or if on-chain state determines allocation

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Whale calls participate(1, maxContributionVirtualAmount) 100 times, contributing 100x the intended maximum. In distribution, they receive a disproportionate share of agent tokens.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All other genesis participants receive diluted allocation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Off-chain distribution could correct, but on-chain state allows over-contribution

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for cumulative contribution exceeding cap

validation_result.mechanism: per-tx cap instead of cumulative per-user cap
validation_result.impact_summary: A user can call Genesis.participate() repeatedly, each time contributing up to maxContributionVirtualAmount, bypassing the intended per-user contribution limit and potentially dominating the genesis allocation.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The Genesis.participate function at line ~130 checks `require(virtualsAmt <= maxContributionVirtualAmount)` which only validates the current transaction's amount. The cumulative mapping `mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender]` is incremented with `+= virtualsAmt` at line ~143 but is never checked against maxContributionVirtualAmount. The code comment itself confirms the intent: 'Check single submission upper limit'. There is no guard preventing multiple calls from the same address, so a user can call participate() N times with maxContributionVirtualAmount each time, accumulating N * maxContributionVirtualAmount total contribution. The `nonReentrant` modifier only prevents reentrancy within a single call, not sequential separate transactions. The `whenActive` modifier only checks the genesis is in an active state. No other mechanism in the contract limits cumulative per-user contributions.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies lines 129-130 (approximately) as the location of the per-transaction check. The code at line ~143 shows the cumulative `+=` without any corresponding cumulative limit check. The variable name `maxContributionVirtualAmount` strongly implies this should be a per-user cap, but the implementation only enforces it per-transaction.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Line 'require(virtualsAmt <= maxContributionVirtualAmount)' only checks the per-transaction amount. The cumulative 'mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender] += virtualsAmt' has no corresponding check like 'require(mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender] + virtualsAmt <= maxContributionVirtualAmount)'. A user can call participate() multiple times, each under the per-tx limit, to accumulate an unlimited total contribution.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls participate(1, maxContributionVirtualAmount) — passes require at line ~130
4. User calls participate(1, maxContributionVirtualAmount) again — passes the same require since it checks only the current tx amount
6. Repeat N times: mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender] = N * maxContributionVirtualAmount
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: Each call to participate() independently passes the per-tx check at line ~130. The cumulative state at line ~143 (mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender] += virtualsAmt) grows without bound relative to maxContributionVirtualAmount. There is no check anywhere in the function that compares the post-increment cumulative value against the cap.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: The fgenesis.js deployment arguments include GENESIS_MAX_CONTRIBUTION_VIRTUAL_AMOUNT as a parameter, confirming maxContributionVirtualAmount is a configurable deployment parameter. The deployGenesis.ts script passes it as params.maxContribution. No deployment script adds any additional off-chain enforcement mechanism.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: A whale with sufficient VIRTUAL tokens calls participate(1, maxContributionVirtualAmount) repeatedly during the active genesis period. Each call passes the per-tx check. After 100 calls, their mapAddrToVirtuals balance is 100x the intended cap. When onGenesisSuccess is called by the OPERATION_ROLE, the off-chain system reads mapAddrToVirtuals to determine allocations. Even if the off-chain system could normalize, the on-chain state records an over-contribution, and the contract holds excess VIRTUAL tokens from this user. The attack requires only sufficient VIRTUAL tokens and gas for multiple transactions.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The defect undermines the fairness mechanism intended by maxContributionVirtualAmount. A well-funded user can dominate genesis participation, potentially receiving a disproportionate share of agent tokens during distribution. While the off-chain OPERATION_ROLE could theoretically normalize distributions, the on-chain contract does not enforce the cap, violating the stated invariant INV-STATE-004. The severity is MEDIUM because: (1) it requires capital, (2) the off-chain distribution could mitigate, but (3) the on-chain code is provably defective.
recommended_mitigations: Change the check from per-transaction to cumulative: require(mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender] + virtualsAmt <= maxContributionVirtualAmount, "Exceeds maximum contribution")
Place the cumulative check BEFORE the state update to follow checks-effects-interactions pattern
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Genesis with maxContributionVirtualAmount = 1000e18. 2. Set up an active genesis (current time between startTime and endTime). 3. Approve sufficient VIRTUAL tokens. 4. Call participate(1, 1000e18) twice from the same address. 5. Assert mapAddrToVirtuals[user] == 2000e18, which exceeds the 1000e18 cap.
uncertainties: Whether the variable name 'maxContributionVirtualAmount' was truly intended as a per-user cap or deliberately a per-transaction cap (the code comment says 'single submission upper limit' suggesting per-tx may be intentional, but this would make the cap largely ineffective)
Whether the off-chain distribution system in OPERATION_ROLE normalizes allocations to mitigate this, though this does not negate the on-chain code defect
additional_testing_needed: Test calling participate() multiple times from the same address and verify cumulative mapAddrToVirtuals exceeds maxContributionVirtualAmount
Test the onGenesisSuccess distribution to see if the off-chain system accounts for over-contributions
